input.background: Each year, cardiologists surgically repair thousands of clogged coronary arteries only to have many of these arteries narrow again (restenose) soon after surgery. A study sponsored by the NIH was conducted to help determine whether infection with a common type of virus, cytomegalovirus (CMY), contributed to coronary restenosis. Forty-nine of the subjects showed serological evidence of CMV infection, while 26 showed no such evidence. In the CMV+ group, 21 individuals restenosed within 6 months of atherectomy. In the CMV — group, 2 individuals restenosed.
input.question: Explain the confidence interval in lay terms.

output.answer: We have 95% confidence in the technique used to calculate the confidence interval. The technique says that CMV increases the risk of restenosis by between 14.7% and 50.2%.

CMV_infection_status.description: Indicates whether the subject showed serological evidence of CMV infection.

restenosis_within_6_months.description: Indicates whether the subject experienced restenosis within 6 months of atherectomy.

number_of_subjects_CMV_positive.description: The number of subjects who showed serological evidence of CMV infection.

number_of_subjects_CMV_negative.description: The number of subjects who showed no serological evidence of CMV infection.

number_of_restenosis_CMV_positive.description: The number of subjects in the CMV+ group who experienced restenosis within 6 months.

number_of_restenosis_CMV_negative.description: The number of subjects in the CMV- group who experienced restenosis within 6 months.
